title: A different take on Shikinami
wikitext:
[]: A different take on Shikinami Post by Bagheera » Sun Dec 19, 2010 7:46 pm Hostile as I am toward Asuka's treatment in Rebuild, I found this thread , and in particular the second post, to be an interesting take on the subject. Assuming his take on the whole affair has merit (and I think it does), what does that say about Shikinami and Rebuild in general? Does the meta nature of her treatment give the plot contortions that enabled it a pass? Would a proper payoff in Q make it all worth it? Now more than ever I don't know what to think. Maybe that's the idea.

[]: Re: A different take on Shikinami Post by Xard » Sun Dec 19, 2010 7:49 pm Bagheera wrote: Hostile as I am toward Asuka's treatment in Rebuild, I found this thread , and in particular the second post, to be an interesting take on the subject. Assuming his take on the whole affair has merit (and I think it does), what does that say about Shikinami and Rebuild in general? Does the meta nature of her treatment give the plot contortions that enabled it a pass? Would a proper payoff in Q make it all worth it? Now more than ever I don't know what to think. Maybe that's the idea.

[]: Post by ran1 » Sun Dec 19, 2010 8:03 pm Don't think so, Tim. At this point "Meta" seems to be everyone's favorite postmodern cop-out for not giving characters due time and development and still justifying it as deep. I mean, sure, everyone loves Tarantino's self-referential antics and him making his characters "meta" in terms of drawing from other sources/film genres, but playing this off as character/narrative depth... This subforum's levels of derp continue to amaze me. Just when I sit back and say to myself -- "Well, I think we've reached the end point in narrative overanalyses" here comes a new theory full of bandwagon adherency. At the end of the day, Rebuild is (I guess?) a visual tour de force in the same way that Tarantino's Kill Bill was. It's definitely Japanese pop art, but adds no edge to it. Macross was innovative insofar as it found a way to glorify the structural narrative in rather unique ways. What I see in Rebuild is first tragedy, then farce. Sadly, Rebuild can't even say it's a multi-genre-pastiche in the way Kill Bill was, which makes 21st Century Anno border on tasteless-hack-with-a-big-budget tier. (I'm looking forward to ensuing argument with Xard at that particular point, I've got my notes ready) I'm almost ready to bracket him in with Steve Segal and Jerry Bruckheimer at this point.

[]: Post by Bagheera » Sun Dec 19, 2010 8:12 pm ^ I understand your frustration, but all you're giving me here is bitterness without substance.

[]: Post by Seele00TextOnly » Sun Dec 19, 2010 8:14 pm I don't really understand what this 'different take' is. I spent a lot of time skimming through that post you linked to and referenced at that other site, but frankly it's just a huge analysis that has little payoff and doesn't seem to show any new perspective on Shikinami to me. I don't understand this thread; perhaps you could quote and elaborate the important parts to you?

[]: Post by esselfortium » Sun Dec 19, 2010 8:18 pm I skimmed the thread and wasn't totally sure what part I was supposed to be looking for. I liked Sankarah's posts, though.

[]: Post by ran1 » Sun Dec 19, 2010 8:38 pm Bagheera wrote: I understand your frustration, but all you're giving me here is bitterness without substance.

[]: Post by Bagheera » Sun Dec 19, 2010 9:28 pm esselfortium wrote: I skimmed the thread and wasn't totally sure what part I was supposed to be looking for. I liked Sankarah's posts, though.

[]: Post by Nonoriri » Sun Dec 19, 2010 9:47 pm But the "Asuka" character existed well before Asuka herself did so that's kind of a non-point, so then to the other point: are we to be reminded that the purpose of her character was to effect Shinji in a not-so-believable way, make a statement on tsundere characters (or whatever nonsense meta interpretation people force on her appearance in 2.0), exist as a decently important character all while her character itself is not actually relevant at all? Additionally I'm wondering what the purpose of a character who exists to embody a statement on character dilution by becoming one of those characters is in a character driven narrative, especially when that purpose exists in opposition to her role as a character. (In that it makes the point but doesn't actually fulfill the basic desire to see a person and not a statement on screen at any given time, which you know is kind of the point)

[]: Post by Bagheera » Sun Dec 19, 2010 10:03 pm ^ Well, yeah. I'm basically in agreement with you here. It's not that I think Shadowjack's analysis is wrong, because I think he's bang on. I just feel like someone got their Excel Saga in my Evangelion, and I don't like it. Metacommentary is fine up to a point, but only as an accent. When it takes over a character's role in the story something is very wrong with the picture.

[]: Post by NAveryW » Sun Dec 19, 2010 10:57 pm The "webcam girl" idea is interesting.

[]: Post by chaosakita » Sun Dec 19, 2010 11:33 pm ran1 wrote: I glorified the shittily composed OP (it's always nice to at least patronize the viewer with a summary, FWIW) with a detailed response (to another post, but still), which is how a forum should work, even with crap posting that's been the trademark of this subforum at times.

[]: Post by esselfortium » Mon Dec 20, 2010 12:55 am While Enokido can't speak for Anno of course, it's made pretty clear from Enokido's interviews in the Complete Records Collection that he thinks Asuka's 2.0 arc was genuinely good and her characterization 'elegant'. That in itself shoots a few major holes in this theory. Besides, turning a character into a generic tsundere to criticize generic tsunderes would probably be toward the top of the list of the most boneheadedly self-defeating exercises in the history of storytelling. Even if that somehow made sense, I don't think Anno is quite foolish enough to be blowing millions of bucks on something he doesn't think works as a story just so he can piss off a few nerds on 2chan. Anyway, it doesn't make a whole lot of sense to consider 2.0 artistic genius if it's just an incestuously self-referential commentary on the original series. That's not cinema, that's onanism.

[]: Post by NAveryW » Mon Dec 20, 2010 2:36 am esselfortium wrote: That's not cinema, that's onanism.

[]: Post by Mr. Tines » Mon Dec 20, 2010 3:21 am A different take? I think that Anno no longer feels Asuka (whatever she was to him) with anything like the same intensity and quality that he did 15 years ago. I can relate to that -- you can't hold something that strong for years. The bits we have seen translated of the Complete Records have shown that including a red-headed German was almost an afterthought; no wonder she only exists as walking plot device and fanservice. ran1 wrote: I'm almost ready to bracket him in with Steve Segal and Jerry Bruckheimer at this point.

[]: Post by GasmaskAvenger » Mon Dec 20, 2010 4:25 am ran1 wrote: I'm almost ready to bracket him in with Steve Segal and Jerry Bruckheimer at this point.

[]: Post by Xard » Mon Dec 20, 2010 6:19 am ran1 wrote: (I'm looking forward to ensuing argument with Xard at that particular point, I've got my notes ready)

[]: Post by darthikari » Mon Dec 20, 2010 9:41 am I know that there's a lot of Shikinami hate going on out there, but bear with me on this point. Let's think about exactly where in the series timeline we actually are. Asuka's defining moments, and by that I mean when we find out her true backstory, happen AFTER the Zuriel fight. Up until these events she was bitching at Rei, flirting with Kaji, and giving Shinji lessons on thermal expantion. I know there is more there, but her facade of strength was pretty much intact until she lost against Zuriel. I'm in the camp of wait-and-see. If anything, I feel that Shikinami has been a bit more human than Soryu. We've been shown a few hints that there's more conflict inside than her TV counterpart displayed by this point. Now I'll be more than happy to eat my words if in Q/Final she runs around in a bunny-girl costume trying to jump Shinji, and her hand puppet gets downgraded to being one of a vast collection of toys she has back in Germany. I'm just holding onto hope that Anno cares more about his property than that. What we've seen so far is narative condensation. We put Asuka in the physical state she was in at the end of EOE, just before the most psychologicaly dammaging point of the TV series. I think that this could be a really good thing....

[]: Post by Naminator » Mon Dec 20, 2010 10:45 am darthikari wrote: Now I'll be more than happy to eat my words if in Q/Final she runs around in a bunny-girl costume trying to jump Shinji

[]: Post by Kendrix » Mon Dec 20, 2010 11:11 am How about trying to understand WHY stuff is different before critizing it just for being different. I actually liked Shikinami's character arc - It's telling us: "Don't be silly, grow up!", it resolves the much fetishized tsundere/female jerkass/ supposed "badass" thing by showing us how childish (essentially what the original series did but in a different way) and turns that unrealistic childish person that wants to be an adult into a TRUE, independent adult not hindered by childish praise. Anno is telling us not do dig assholes for assholes' sake because he was portraying said asshole for the purpose of showing that she was an asshole - look at all this comments about our society having to "grow up" and his work being "misunderstood" NGE was telling us that tsunderes suck by exaggerating one, NME is telling us by having one become a better human being Oh well, that's just my interpretation of Anno's intention that doesn't necessarily have to be right, NOT an attempt to insult anyone.
